findings.finding: List renders from SDK state but edit/delete operates on separate electron-store — divergent data sources with no reconciliation
findings.description: The server list (items memo, line 108) renders from sync.data.mcp, populated by sdk.mcp.status() via TanStack Query (server-sync.tsx:52). But editing (handleEdit, line 67) and deleting (confirmRemove, line 92) read/write from electron-store via api.getMcpServers/setMcpServers. These are two completely independent data stores with different schemas and lifetimes. A server can: (a) appear in the list but fail to edit (not in store), (b) be editable but invisible (in store but removed from SDK), or (c) be deleted from one but reappear from the other on refetch. The only reconciliation point is when the user opens the dialog — there's no sync or migration between the two stores.
findings.reproducible:
1. Add server via SDK directly (bypassing electron-store)
2. Open MCP dialog — server appears in list (from sync.data.mcp)
3. Click Edit — handleEdit reads from electron-store, server not found → edit silently fails
findings.repair_instruction: Add a reconciliation step: when handleEdit finds a server in sync.data.mcp that's not in electron-store, fall back to constructing a default entry from available SDK data. Better: unify the data source to always read from electron-store and use SDK status only for status indicators. Or add a sync function that merges SDK state into electron-store on dialog open.

findings.finding: DialogEditMcp's useDialog().close() may close the outer DialogSelectMcp instead of the edit overlay — nested Kobalte dialog portal conflict
findings.description: DialogEditMcp uses useDialog() from @opencode-ai/ui/context/dialog, which manages a dialog stack via SolidJS context. DialogEditMcp is rendered inside DialogSelectMcp which also uses the same dialog context. When DialogEditMcp calls dialog.close() (line 57 on Save, line 127 on Cancel), the dialog context's stack-based close() may close the outermost dialog (DialogSelectMcp) instead of just the inner edit overlay. This is because Kobalte's dialog close disposes the createRoot of the currently active portal, which may be the outer dialog if the inner one wasn't properly stacked. The nested Dialog-within-Dialog architecture creates competing portal layers with potential z-index, focus trap, and Escape key conflicts.
findings.reproducible:
1. Open MCP dialog (DialogSelectMcp)
2. Click Edit on a server → DialogEditMcp opens
3. Click Save or Cancel → entire MCP management dialog may close (not just the edit overlay)
findings.repair_instruction: Restructure to avoid nested dialogs. Options: (a) render DialogEditMcp as an inline drawer/panel instead of a separate Dialog component, (b) use a separate dialog context or portal for the edit dialog, or (c) replace the content of DialogSelectMcp with the edit form instead of overlaying a second dialog. Test with Kobalte dialog context to verify close() target.

findings.finding: addMutation only supports remote MCP server type — no UI path to add local servers
findings.description: The inline 'Add MCP Server' form always constructs an McpRemoteConfig with type:'remote' and a URL. There is no UI field or toggle for adding local MCP servers (command + args). DialogEditMcp supports both local and remote via its entry prop, but the add form — the only path for creating new servers in this dialog — only produces remote entries.
findings.reproducible:
1. Open MCP dialog
2. Click 'Add MCP Server'
3. Form only has name and URL fields — no command/args, no local type option
findings.repair_instruction: Add a type toggle (local/remote) to the add form, or reuse DialogEditMcp for both add and edit flows. The add mode in DialogSelectMcp should either be replaced by using DialogEditMcp in create mode, or extended to support both types.

findings.finding: toggle mutation has no onError handler — connect/authenticate failures are silently swallowed
findings.description: The toggle mutation (lines 112-127) defines mutationFn that calls sdk.client.mcp.disconnect, sdk.client.mcp.auth.authenticate, or sdk.client.mcp.connect. It only has an onSuccess handler that refetches queries. There is NO onError handler. If connect or authenticate throws (network error, auth failure, server unreachable), the error propagates to TanStack Query where it's stored internally but never surfaced to the user. The toggle button appears to do nothing — no toast, no error feedback, no status change.
findings.reproducible:
1. Server is disconnected
2. Click toggle to connect
3. SDK throws (server unreachable)
4. No error shown to user — toggle appears to have no effect

findings.finding: resetForm allows escape from pending addMutation — user can interact with list while server add is in flight
findings.description: resetForm() at line 169 unconditionally sets store.mode to 'list' without checking addMutation.isPending. The user can submit an add mutation, then click the back arrow (line 205) or press Escape (line 192) to return to list mode while the add is still in flight. The list becomes interactive — user can edit or delete servers — while the background addMutation may still be resolving. If addMutation.onSuccess fires (line 138-142), it resets the addForm (harmless) and refetches queries, potentially disrupting in-progress list interactions.
findings.reproducible:
1. Fill add form with name/URL
2. Click Add → addMutation starts
3. While addMutation.isPending is true, click back arrow → resetForm() runs
4. List becomes visible, user can interact with servers
5. addMutation completes, refetchQueries fires — list updates under user's cursor
findings.repair_instruction: Guard resetForm: if (addMutation.isPending) return before setting mode to 'list'. Alternatively, show loading spinner instead of allowing navigation away from the form while mutation is pending.

findings.finding: IPC set-desktop-mcp-servers handler has no try/catch — disk errors become unhandled promise rejections
findings.description: The set-desktop-mcp-servers IPC handler directly calls store.set('servers', ...) without any try/catch. If electron-store encounters a disk error (permissions, disk full, corrupt file), the exception becomes an unhandled Promise rejection in the renderer process. The renderer's confirmRemove function catches errors in its own scope (line 95), but the error from the IPC handler would be thrown before that — causing an uncaught renderer error.
findings.reproducible:
1. Fill disk to capacity or set store file to read-only
2. Delete an MCP server
3. confirmRemove calls api.setMcpServers (line 94) → IPC handler throws → unhandled promise rejection

findings.finding: pathKey(sync.directory) can crash if sync.directory is null — no guard in multiple locations
findings.description: pathKey(sync.directory) is called at lines 99, 127, 141, and 156 without a null/undefined guard. The pathKey function at packages/app/src/utils/path-key.ts indexes into the string with [1] early in its implementation. If sync.directory is null, pathKey(null) would crash inside a createMemo or mutation callback, potentially taking down the entire component or worse, crashing in the mutation callback without easy recovery.
findings.reproducible: If sync.directory is null/undefined at any of the 4 call sites, pathKey crashes with TypeError. This is most likely during initial load or project switching.
findings.repair_instruction: Add null guard: const dir = sync.directory; if (!dir) return; before computing pathKey. Or inline: pathKey(sync.directory ?? '') to use empty string as fallback.

findings.finding: No URL validation in dialog-edit-mcp — arbitrary protocol/dangerous values accepted
findings.description: The URL TextField accepts any input string with no validation. The user can enter 'javascript:alert(1)', 'data:text/html,...', or arbitrary garbage. While the SDK will eventually reject invalid URLs, the UI provides no immediate feedback and allows invalid data through the form.
findings.reproducible:
1. Open edit dialog for server (remote type)
2. Enter 'not-a-url' in URL field
3. Click Save — no validation, entry created with invalid URL
findings.repair_instruction: Add URL validation before saving: try { new URL(url()) } catch { show error }. Alternatively use a URL-input component with built-in validation.

findings.finding: Zero timeout value is silently stored — likely unintended configuration
findings.description: If user types '0' in the timeout field, Number('0') returns 0 and the guard passes (0 !== NaN, 0 !== undefined). A timeout of 0 milliseconds means 'timeout immediately' — functionally the same as a broken server. The user likely didn't intend to set a 0ms timeout. The HTML attributes include min={0} (line 112) which permits 0.
findings.reproducible:
1. Open edit dialog for local server
2. Type '0' in timeout field
3. Save
4. Entry stored with timeout: 0
findings.repair_instruction: Set min={1} on the timeout TextField and/or add validation: if (timeoutVal <= 0) show error 'Timeout must be greater than 0'.

findings.finding: Type switch in dialog-edit-mcp cross-contaminates form fields — stale values persist across type toggle
findings.description: When switching type from 'local' to 'remote' then back to 'local', the command and timeout signals retain their original values (initialized at mount time). This is intentional for resuming edits but can be surprising if user expects fields to reset when switching type. More critically: if user edits a local server with command 'node server.js', switches to remote to enter a URL, then saves (remote type selected) — the command value is silently discarded (correct for remote) but the user may not realize the switch discarded their command entry.
findings.reproducible:
1. Open edit for existing local server
2. Clear command, switch to remote, enter URL
3. Switch back to local — command field shows the ORIGINAL value from mount time
4. If the user had cleared command before switching, switching back shows the original stale command
findings.repair_instruction: Add field reset logic when type changes: on setType('local'), clear url() if empty; on setType('remote'), clear command() and setTimeoutValue(''). Or preserve user intent by tracking what was typed, not what was loaded.

findings.finding: Edit onSave handler silently discards all edits — no persistence path exists
findings.description: The onSave handler for DialogEditMcp at line 191-193 only calls setEditingEntry(undefined) — it never persists the edited entry to electron-store or calls any SDK mutation. The DialogEditMcp's handleSave creates a valid McpServerEntry object with all edits and passes it to onSave, but the parent discards it. The user sees the dialog close and no error, but the server configuration is unchanged. This is a silent no-op that makes the entire edit feature non-functional.
findings.reproducible:
1. Open MCP dialog
2. Click Edit on any server
3. Change name/timeout/command
4. Click Save
5. Dialog closes, no error
6. Open Edit again — changes are gone
findings.repair_instruction:
Add an editMutation useMutation that:
1. Reads current servers from electron-store via api.getMcpServers()
2. Replaces the edited entry (matched by name) in the array
3. Writes back via api.setMcpServers()
4. Calls sdk.client.mcp.add?.(...) or sdk.client.mcp.update?.(...) to sync with SDK
5. Refetches queries on success

Alternatively, wire the onSave handler to perform these operations directly in dialog-select-mcp.tsx:191-193.

findings.finding: editingEntry signal never cleared when user cancels edit — causes stale state on subsequent edit
findings.description: setEditingEntry(undefined) is only called in the onSave handler. When user clicks Cancel in DialogEditMcp, dialog.close() is called but editingEntry signal still holds the reference. On next edit, if DialogEditMcp is reused without remount (SolidJS behavior), internal createSignal calls capture the OLD props.entry value, causing the form to show stale data from the first server.
findings.reproducible:
1. Click Edit on server-A → DialogEditMcp opens with server-A data
2. Click Cancel → dialog closes
3. Click Edit on server-B → DialogEditMcp opens but shows server-A's name (if component not remounted)
findings.repair_instruction: Add a onCancel prop to DialogEditMcp or use onCleanup to call setEditingEntry(undefined) when the component unmounts. Alternatively, use SolidJS createMemo or createRenderEffect inside DialogEditMcp instead of createSignal for the form fields so they react to prop changes.

findings.finding: DialogEditMcp createSignal captures initial props.entry once — signals don't react to prop changes
findings.description: DialogEditMcp uses createSignal(props.entry?.name ?? '') at the top level of the component function. SolidJS components execute once — createSignal evaluates the initial value at creation time only. When editingEntry changes (e.g., from server A to server B), the component receives new props but the signals retain the original values. This means: edit server A, cancel, edit server B → form still shows server A's name/command/timeout. Switch type toggle compounds the issue because stale command/url/timeout fields persist across type switches.
findings.reproducible:
1. Click Edit on server-A (name: 'a-server', command: ['node', 'a.js'])
2. Click Cancel
3. Click Edit on server-B (name: 'b-server', command: ['node', 'b.js'])
4. Form shows 'a-server' and 'node a.js' instead of server-B's values
findings.repair_instruction:
Replace createSignal with reactive patterns:
- Use createMemo(() => props.entry?.name ?? '') for read-only display
- Or wire form fields directly to props.entry with createRenderEffect to sync on prop change
- Or use a SolidJS store that resets when props.entry changes
- Or pass a 'key' prop to force remount on entry change

findings.finding: confirmRemove lacks execution guard — rapid double-click triggers concurrent operations
findings.description: confirmRemove is async but has no isPending flag or mutex guard. If user double-clicks the Delete button, confirmRemove runs twice concurrently. First call: disconnect → auth.remove → store remove. Second call: disconnect on already-disconnected server (may throw, but caught by empty try/catch), auth.remove (already removed, may throw), store.filter finds nothing (no-op). The refetchQueries at the end runs twice. No data corruption is guaranteed, but the second call's filtered server list could be stale if a concurrent write happened between the calls.
findings.reproducible:
1. Click Delete on any server
2. Click the Delete confirmation button rapidly twice
3. confirmRemove runs twice — second call's disconnect throws (caught), second store removal is no-op
4. No visible harm but unstable state during execution

findings.finding: isValidMcpEntry allows empty name strings to be stored
findings.description: isValidMcpEntry checks `typeof obj.name !== 'string'` which rejects non-strings but passes empty string ''. A server with name '' can be written to electron-store via set-desktop-mcp-servers. The UI uses name as a React key and display value — empty name causes blank entries in the server list and potential breakage in List component (key='').
findings.reproducible:
1. Call api.setMcpServers([{name: '', config: {type: 'local', command: ['node'], enabled: true}}])
2. Entry is written to store
3. get-desktop-mcp-servers returns the entry (passes validation)
4. UI renders entry with empty/blank name
findings.repair_instruction: Add `if (typeof obj.name !== 'string' || obj.name.trim() === '') return false` to isValidMcpEntry.

findings.finding: isValidMcpEntry allows empty command array for local-type servers
findings.description: The check `!Array.isArray(config.command)` passes for empty arrays because `Array.isArray([])` is true. A server with `command: []` is stored validly but cannot actually start — no executable specified. The dialog-edit-mcp produces empty arrays when user leaves command field empty: `"".split(/\s+/).filter(Boolean)` = `[]`.
findings.reproducible:
1. In DialogEditMcp, leave command field empty, fill timeout, save
2. Entry stored with command: []3. Server appears in list but cannot be started
findings.repair_instruction: Add minimum array length check: `if (config.type === 'local' && (!Array.isArray(config.command) || config.command.length === 0)) return false`

findings.finding: isValidMcpEntry accepts unknown config types that aren't 'local' or 'remote'
findings.description: The return statement `return config.type === 'local' || config.type === 'remote'` correctly rejects unknown types. But the branching logic before it ONLY validates command/url when the type matches. If config.type is 'hybrid' (unknown), it passes the type-specific checks (because neither condition is true) but fails at the final return. This is actually correct — unknown types are rejected. But there's no warning about why the entry was rejected (just generic 'Dropping invalid MCP server entry'). This is informational, not a bug.
findings.reproducible: → Not a bug. Included for completeness.
findings.repair_instruction: N/A — validation works correctly for unknown types.

findings.finding: Whitespace-only server name passes through dialog-edit-mcp save
findings.description: The handleSave function assigns `name: name()` without trimming whitespace. If user enters '   ' (spaces only), `name()` = '   ', which is truthy, so the disabled guard `!name()` doesn't catch it. The entry is created with a whitespace-only name. This passes isValidMcpEntry (typeof name === 'string' is true) and gets stored in electron-store. The UI renders the whitespace name, which looks like an empty entry.
findings.reproducible:
1. Open DialogEditMcp
2. Enter '   ' for name
3. Enter a valid command
4. Click Save
5. Entry stored with whitespace name
findings.repair_instruction: Add trim to the name field in handleSave: `const trimmedName = name().trim(); if (!trimmedName) return;` and use trimmedName in the entry object.

findings.finding: Negative timeout values pass through dialog-edit-mcp and are stored
findings.description: The timeout field is type='number' with min={0}, but this only affects the browser's step UI — it doesn't prevent manual typing of negative values. `Number('-5')` returns -5, `Number.isNaN(-5)` is false, and the guard `!Number.isNaN(timeoutVal)` passes. Negative timeout is stored as `timeout: -5` and could cause undefined behavior in the MCP SDK (timeout already expired before start).
findings.reproducible:
1. Open edit dialog for local server
2. Type '-5000' in timeout field
3. Save
4. Entry stored with timeout: -5000
findings.repair_instruction: Add min/max validation: `if (timeoutVal < 0 || timeoutVal > MAX_TIMEOUT) timeoutVal = undefined` before including in entry.

findings.finding: validateAndFilterMcpServers silently drops all entries with only console.warn — no user notification
findings.description: If ALL servers in the array are invalid (e.g., after a data migration or corruption), validateAndFilterMcpServers returns an empty array with only a console.warn per entry. The caller then sets this empty array to electron-store, effectively wiping all MCP server configurations. The user sees an empty server list with no error message, toast, or explanation. This is especially dangerous during write: the same filter is applied on write, so if client-side code corrupts an entry, the next save silently loses data.
findings.reproducible:
1. Have 5 valid MCP servers
2. A bug causes one entry to become malformed in the array before set-desktop-mcp-servers
3. The malformed entry is dropped (console.warn)4. The 4 valid entries are saved
Only 1 lost — but if data format changes, ALL could become invalid and ALL are dropped silently
findings.repair_instruction: Add a non-silent fallback: if filtered length < original length, log a structured warning event. If filtered length is 0 while original length > 0, throw an error instead of destroying data. Or keep original data and warn instead of setting empty array.

findings.finding: confirmRemove and toggle mutation race on the same server's disconnect
findings.description: The toggle mutation (line 112-124) reads sync.data.mcp[name] status and calls disconnect for connected servers. confirmRemove (line 77-96) also calls disconnect. If user toggles a server OFF and then immediately clicks Delete, both code paths call `sdk.client.mcp.disconnect({name})` concurrently. The second disconnect may throw (server already disconnecting or disconnected), but both calls wrap in try/catch. Worse: the store removal in confirmRemove happens independently of toggle's resolution, potentially removing a server that toggle is still operating on.
findings.reproducible:
1. Server is connected
2. Click toggle to disconnect
3. Before toggle resolves, click Delete, then Delete confirm
4. Two disconnect calls race
findings.repair_instruction: Use a shared mutex or signal for the delete flow that blocks toggle operations. Or check toggle.isPending in confirmRemove and skip if toggle is active for the same server.

findings.finding: Empty catch block on auth.remove swallows ALL errors, not just 'method not found'
findings.description: The empty catch block at line 87 was intended to suppress 'method not found' errors when auth.remove doesn't exist on older SDK versions. However, it catches ALL errors: network failures, auth failures, permission errors. A failing auth.remove could leave the server in a state where auth credentials are still stored while the server is removed from the store — an orphaned credential leak.
findings.reproducible:
1. SDK client has auth.remove but it fails (network error)
2. confirmRemove continues silently
3. Server config removed from store but auth credentials remain on disk
findings.repair_instruction: Change to specific error discrimination: catch only if error is 'method not found' (check error.code or error.message). Re-throw other errors, or at minimum console.warn with structured context.

findings.finding: handleEdit double-call race — second call can win if responses arrive out of order
findings.description: handleEdit is async: it fetches servers from electron-store, finds the entry, and sets editingEntry. If user clicks Edit on server-A, then immediately clicks Edit on server-B (before first resolves), two API calls are in flight. If server-B's response arrives before server-A's response, editingEntry is correctly set to server-B. But then server-A's response arrives last and overwrites editingEntry to server-A. The user sees the edit dialog for server-A even though they last clicked server-B.
findings.reproducible:
1. Two MCP servers exist
2. Click Edit on server-A
3. IMMEDIATELY click Edit on server-B
4. If server-A's response is slower, dialog shows server-B correctly
5. If server-A's response arrives last, dialog switches to server-A (wrong)
findings.repair_instruction: Add an abort pattern: track the active edit request with a counter or AbortController. Only apply setEditingEntry if the response matches the latest request.

findings.finding: IPC set-desktop-mcp-servers concurrent writes cause last-write-wins data loss
findings.description:
The set-desktop-mcp-servers IPC handler reads the filter input and writes directly. If two renderer processes or two rapid calls happen concurrently:
1. Call A reads servers: [A, B, C]
2. Call B reads servers: [A, B, C]
3. Call A removes server A, writes: [B, C]
4. Call B adds server D, writes: [A, B, C, D] (correct for B's perspective, but overwrites A's removal)
5. Server A is resurrected, server B and C are duplicated. This is a classic read-modify-write race.
findings.reproducible: Two concurrent IPC calls to set-desktop-mcp-servers with different mutations can overwrite each other.
findings.repair_instruction: Add a write queue or use electron-store's built-in mutex. Alternative: use a single mutation IPC that takes an action (add/remove/update) rather than replacing the entire array, and let the IPC handler perform the read-update-write atomically.

findings.finding: disconnectMutation is defined but never used — dead code
findings.description: A useMutation named disconnectMutation is defined at line 149-155 that calls disconnect for connected servers. It has an onSuccess handler that refetches queries. However, this mutation is never called anywhere in the component template or logic. It is dead code — likely a leftover from an earlier refactor. The toggle mutation (line 112) handles disconnect functionality instead.
findings.reproducible: Static analysis: grep for 'disconnectMutation' in file — only definition, no calls.
findings.repair_instruction: Remove the unused disconnectMutation to eliminate confusion. If it's intended for future use, add a code comment explaining the planned usage.

findings.finding: No user-facing error feedback when handleEdit fails silently
findings.description: When api.getMcpServers is unavailable or throws, handleEdit catches the error and only calls console.warn. The user clicks Edit but nothing happens — no toast, no error indicator. This is confusing because the dropdown menu item appears to work but the edit dialog doesn't open.
findings.reproducible:
1. Preload script doesn't expose api.getMcpServers
2. User clicks Edit on any server
3. Nothing visible happens
findings.repair_instruction: Show a toast notification on failure: showToast({ variant: 'error', title: 'Failed to open editor', description: error message })

findings.finding: No user feedback when store removal fails in confirmRemove
findings.description: If api.setMcpServers throws (write failure), the error is caught and console.warn'd. The server has already been disconnected and auth removed, but is still in the store. The user sees the confirmation dialog close and the item might still appear in the list after refetch. No toast or error shown.
findings.reproducible:
1. Trigger a write failure (disk full, permission error)
2. Delete a server
3. Server disconnected, auth removed, but still in store
4. Refetch runs — server may reappear (but disconnected)
findings.repair_instruction: Add toast notification on store write failure. Consider reversing the order: remove from store FIRST, then disconnect/auth (though this has opposite risk — store removed but disconnect fails = orphaned connection).

findings.finding: Inline add form and edit dialog can be simultaneously active — conflicting UI states
findings.description: store.mode controls form mode ('list' | 'add'). editingEntry controls whether the edit dialog shows. When user clicks 'Add MCP Server', mode switches to 'add', showing the add form (fallback of Show when={!isFormMode()}). But editingEntry can still be set via handleEdit called from the dropdown menu while in add mode, which would render DialogEditMcp OVER the add form. The edit dialog would appear as an overlay while the add form is still showing — two conflicting interactions.
findings.reproducible:
1. Click 'Add MCP Server' → add form shows
2. From the search results or dropdown, click Edit on an existing server
3. DialogEditMcp overlays on top of add form
4. Two dialogs active simultaneously
findings.repair_instruction: Guard handleEdit to not run when store.mode !== 'list'. Or switch mode back to 'list' when edit is triggered.

findings.finding: confirmRemove closure captures stale confirmDelete value — if cancelDelete is called mid-execution, UI state desyncs
findings.description: confirmRemove captures `const name = confirmDelete()` at the start. If cancelDelete is called between the disconnect and store removal await points, `confirmDelete()` returns undefined for subsequent calls, but the captured `name` variable is already set. The operation continues to completion, but the confirmation UI disappears (cancelDelete set it to undefined) while the async operations are still running. The user sees the confirmation vanish but then the server disappears from the list a moment later — confusing UX.
findings.reproducible:
1. Click Delete → confirm dialog shows
2. Click Delete (starts confirmRemove)
3. While disconnect is in flight, quickly click Cancel
4. Confirm dialog disappears
5. Server still gets deleted (operation completes)
findings.repair_instruction: Make confirmRemove check that confirmDelete() still matches the operation target at each await boundary. Or disable the Cancel button once Delete is clicked.

findings.finding: (window as any).api bypass — no type safety on IPC boundary
findings.description: The IPC bridge is accessed via `(window as any).api` with optional chaining. No TypeScript types are shared between the preload script and the renderer code. If the preload script changes a method name (e.g., getMcpServers → getDesktopMcpServers), there's no compile-time error — only a silent runtime failure.
findings.reproducible: Rename api.getMcpServers in preload script without updating dialog-select-mcp.tsx → handleEdit silently fails
findings.repair_instruction: Define a shared type for the preload API interface (e.g., DesktopApi) and type window.api with it. Import the type in dialog-select-mcp.tsx.

findings.finding: Float timeout values pass through dialog-edit-mcp without rounding — stored as-is
findings.description: Number('1.5') returns 1.5 which passes the NaN check and is stored as timeout: 1.5. While the type definition says 'timeout?: number', a float value could cause unexpected behavior in SDK/OS APIs that expect integer milliseconds. The HTML input type='number' with step='1' would help but is not set.
findings.reproducible:
1. Open edit dialog for local server
2. Type '1.5' in timeout field
3. Save → entry stored with timeout: 1.5
findings.repair_instruction: Use Number.isInteger() check or Math.round(timeoutVal) to ensure integer timeout values.

findings.finding: addMutation onSuccess resets form but doesn't wait for refetch — stale data briefly visible
findings.description: The onSuccess handler immediately sets mode to 'list' and resets the form, then calls refetchQueries. There's a brief window where the list is shown but the query data hasn't refreshed yet — the newly added server won't appear until refetch completes. If the user has many servers or slow IPC, this delay is noticeable.
findings.reproducible:
1. Add a new server via inline form
2. onSuccess fires → mode switches to 'list' immediately
3. List shows for 50-500ms without the new server
4. Refetch completes → new server appears
findings.repair_instruction: Await the refetch before switching mode: `await queryClient.refetchQueries(...); setStore('mode', 'list')`. Make onSuccess async.

findings.finding: addMutation onError shows raw error to user — may expose internal details
findings.description: The onError handler extracts `err instanceof Error ? err.message : String(err)` and sets it as form error text. If the SDK returns verbose internal errors, these are displayed directly to the user without sanitization.
findings.reproducible: Trigger a backend error that returns internal stack traces in the message
findings.repair_instruction: Sanitize error messages: map known error patterns to user-friendly messages, fall back to 'An unexpected error occurred' for unknown patterns.

findings.finding: List component key uses fallback '' — duplicate empty keys possible
findings.description: The List component key is `(x) => x?.name ?? ''`. If two entries have empty string names (allowed by validation gap F-005), both get key '' which causes SolidJS reconciliation issues.
findings.reproducible: Add two server entries with empty name → List component has duplicate key ''
findings.repair_instruction: Use a more unique key: `x?.name ?? crypto.randomUUID()`. But F-005 (validate empty name) is the root cause.

findings.finding: Query cache accumulates entries per directory — potential growth with directory switching
findings.description: refetchQueries uses queryOptions.mcp(pathKey(sync.directory)) as the query filter. If the user switches directories (changing sync.directory), query keys accumulate in the cache for old directories. Each directory switch creates a new cache entry that is never invalidated or garbage collected.
findings.reproducible:
1. Open project in directory A → query cache entry for pathKey(A)
2. Switch to directory B → cache entry for pathKey(B)
3. Switch back to A → cache hit for pathKey(A)
4. Each unique directory creates a permanent cache entry
findings.repair_instruction: Set a staleTime or gcTime on the query options to evict old directory entries, or subscribe to directory changes and actively remove stale query keys.

findings.finding: dialog-edit-mcp handleSave doesn't guard against double-submit via enter key
findings.description: handleSave has no isPending guard. If the parent onSave callback becomes async in the future, double-clicking Save could trigger two saves. Currently onSave only sets editingEntry(undefined) synchronously, so the second call would just set undefined again — no-op. But if onSave becomes async (F-001 repair), this becomes a race condition.
findings.reproducible: Only relevant after F-001 repair. Currently non-issue because onSave is synchronous and idempotent.
findings.repair_instruction: Add a useMutation or isSaving signal in DialogEditMcp to gate handleSave and disable the Save button while saving.

findings.finding: handleEdit fetch bypasses TanStack Query cache — direct electron-store access
findings.description: handleEdit fetches server data directly from electron-store via window.api.getMcpServers() instead of using the query cache from useQueryOptions. If the query cache has fresher data (e.g., from addMutation's refetch), handleEdit may show stale data that lags behind the query cache.
findings.reproducible:
1. Query cache has latest server list from refetch
2. handleEdit fetches directly from electron-store (might be behind cache)
3. Edit dialog shows stale data if cache is ahead of store
findings.repair_instruction: Consider using queryClient.getQueryData(queryOptions.mcp(pathKey(sync.directory))) to read from the query cache instead of making a direct IPC call.

findings.finding: ipcMain.handle registrations are not removed on app teardown — hot-reload hazard
findings.description: registerIpcHandlers uses ipcMain.handle to register handlers. If this function is called twice (dev hot-reload, testing framework), Electron throws 'Error: The handler for channel 'xxx' has already been registered'. There's no removeIpcHandlers or cleanup mechanism.
findings.reproducible: Call registerIpcHandlers twice → Electron throws on duplicate handler registration
findings.repair_instruction: Either call registerIpcHandlers only once and extract per-handler registration, or add ipcMain.removeHandler calls before registration.

findings.finding: McpServerConfig union type lacks discriminated type narrowing in dialog-edit-mcp
findings.description: DialogEditMcp constructs McpServerEntry with local or remote config based on type() signal, but the conditional branches manually construct the config shape rather than relying on TypeScript narrowing. If a new type is added to McpServerConfig, this code won't warn at compile-time that the switch is incomplete.
findings.reproducible: Add a third config type 'stdio' to McpServerConfig — dialog-edit-mcp handleSave only handles 'local' and 'remote' with no exhaustiveness check
findings.repair_instruction: Use a discriminated switch with a compile-time exhaustiveness check via `const _exhaustive: never = type` in the default branch.

findings.finding: items() memo uses sync.data.mcp ?? {} fallback but doesn't handle null entries
findings.description: The memo maps over Object.entries(sync.data.mcp ?? {}). If an entry in the mcp object has a null or undefined value, `status.status` would throw. However, the SDK contract should guarantee well-formed entries. The null fallback for mcp covers startup/loading state.
findings.reproducible: An SDK sync that includes a server name with null status would crash the items() memo
findings.repair_instruction: Add optional chaining: `.map(([name, status]) => ({ name, status: status?.status }))`

most_critical.F-001: Edit flow is a no-op — onSave handler discards all edits without persistence
most_critical.F-002: editingEntry signal leaks — cancel doesn't clear it, causing stale state on next edit
most_critical.F-003: DialogEditMcp signals capture initial props once — component shows stale data when entry changes
most_critical.F-031: Dual source of truth — list from SDK, edit/delete from electron-store, no reconciliation
most_critical.F-032: Nested Kobalte dialog portals — dialog.close() may close the outer dialog instead of the edit overlay

subagent_contributions.edge-case-enumerator: EC-11 (blocker: editingEntry never cleared), EC-18 (blocker: stale signals), EC-20 (major: dialog.close closes outer), EC-33 (major: nested portals), EC-34 (major: add only remote), EC-19 (major: no URL validation)
subagent_contributions.state-poisoner: F2 (blocker: dual source of truth), F4 (major: resetForm no guard), Sequences 3, 7, 8, 9
subagent_contributions.dependency-saboteur: sdk.client.connect/authenticate no onError (major), pathKey(null) crash (major), store.set no try/catch (major)
subagent_contributions.concurrency-stresser: Race 2 (confirmRemove+cancelDelete), Race 4 (Edit+Edit overwrite), Race 5 (IPC concurrent writes), Race 9 (edit no-op save)
subagent_contributions.memory-leak-hunter: editingEntry never cleared, confirmDelete error path, DialogEditMcp signal stale state, IPC double-registration hazard
subagent_contributions.assumption-challenger: Falsified 10 of 15 assumptions — 2 High severity (signals not reactive, Show not remounting), 5 Medium severity
